def: Loss of the ability to feel pleasure in normally enjoyable activities; a core depressive and negative symptom.
A marked lack of motivation to start and sustain purposeful activity; a negative symptom of schizophrenia.
Reduced or absent outward display of emotion in face and voice; a negative symptom of schizophrenia.
Reduced amount or content of spontaneous speech; a negative symptom of schizophrenia.
Visible slowing of movement, speech and thought; a feature of severe depression.
A distressing inner restlessness with an urge to move; a common, often early antipsychotic side effect treated with propranolol.
Sudden sustained muscle contractions (neck twisting, eyes rolling up) from dopamine blockade; treated with anticholinergics like benztropine.
An acute dystonic reaction with forced, sustained upward deviation of the eyes; a dopamine-blocker side effect.
Tremor, rigidity and slowed movement caused by dopamine-blocking drugs, mimicking Parkinson disease; anticholinergics help.
Late-onset, often irreversible involuntary movements (esp. tongue, lips, face) from long-term dopamine blockade; anticholinergics worsen it.
Brief, irregular, dance-like involuntary movements that flow between body parts; seen in Huntington disease.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; in psychiatry often from lithium or SSRIs, or as an essential/physiological tremor.
A drop in blood pressure on standing, causing light-headedness or fainting; common with alpha-1-blocking drugs.
Excessive sweating; a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and adrenergic states.
Exaggerated, brisk deep-tendon reflexes; a hallmark of serotonin syndrome, in contrast to the hyporeflexia of NMS.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions on stretch (e.g., at ankle or eyes); a key sign of serotonin toxicity.
Dangerously elevated body temperature; a cardinal feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and malignant hyperthermia.
Uniform, sustained muscle stiffness through the whole range of motion; the classic rigidity of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Pupil dilation; part of the anticholinergic toxidrome ('blind as a bat') and adrenergic states.
A heightened, sustained scanning for threat; a hyperarousal symptom of PTSD.
A PTSD symptom cluster of heightened arousal: hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, irritability, and poor concentration.
Worsening confusion and agitation in the late afternoon or evening, seen in delirium and dementia.
Impaired coordination and unsteady gait; a common effect of benzodiazepines and drug toxicity.
Abnormal tingling or 'pins and needles' sensation; a topiramate effect stemming from carbonic-anhydrase inhibition.
A perception without an external stimulus, such as hearing voices or seeing things that are not there.
A fixed false belief firmly held despite clear contrary evidence; a core feature of psychosis.
The 'absent' symptoms of schizophrenia: flat affect, poverty of speech, anhedonia, avolition, social withdrawal; often the most disabling.
The 'added' symptoms of psychosis: delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized thought or speech.
Excessive drooling or salivation; a characteristic clozapine side effect.
A persistent, often painful erection unrelated to arousal; a urologic emergency linked to alpha-1 blockade (e.g., trazodone).
Difficulty emptying the bladder; part of the anticholinergic toxidrome ('full as a flask') and a noradrenergic effect.
Inability to form new memories for a period; caused by benzodiazepines and Z-drugs such as triazolam and midazolam.
Temporary inability to move when falling asleep or waking; can be triggered by orexin-antagonist hypnotics.
Sudden airway constriction causing wheeze and breathlessness; non-selective beta-blockers can trigger it in asthma or COPD.
A potentially fatal serotonergic excess: agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia and autonomic instability; onset in hours.
A rare dopamine-blocker reaction over days: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, autonomic instability and raised CK.
Drug-induced movement disorders from dopamine blockade: acute dystonia, parkinsonism and akathisia.
Flu-like symptoms, dizziness and 'brain zaps' after abruptly stopping an antidepressant; worse with short-half-life agents like paroxetine.
The cluster of weight gain, dyslipidemia and high glucose driven by some antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and clozapine.
Muscarinic-blockade toxidrome: dry, flushed, hot skin, mydriasis, urinary retention, delirium and tachycardia.
Toxic lithium levels causing tremor, GI upset, ataxia, confusion and, if severe, seizures; often triggered by dehydration or renal loss.
A hypertensive emergency when MAOIs meet tyramine-rich foods; a norepinephrine surge with a sudden pounding occipital headache.
Lengthening of the heart's QT interval on ECG, raising the risk of the dangerous arrhythmia torsades de pointes.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia arising from a prolonged QT interval; can be fatal.
A dangerous drop in neutrophils causing severe infection risk; the reason clozapine requires routine blood-count monitoring.
A severe, potentially fatal drug reaction with skin blistering and mucosal loss; the lamotrigine and carbamazepine rash warning.
Yellowing of skin and eyes from impaired bile flow; a distinctive chlorpromazine adverse effect.
Drug-induced liver damage, signalled by jaundice or rising liver enzymes; a concern with atomoxetine and duloxetine.
Dangerously slowed, shallow breathing; the lethal risk when opioids, benzodiazepines or alcohol are combined.
A temporary worsening of insomnia beyond baseline after stopping a hypnotic.
A sharp rise in blood pressure after abruptly stopping an alpha-2 agonist like clonidine or guanfacine; prevent by tapering.
A rare inherited reaction to certain anaesthetics causing extreme hyperthermia and rigidity; a differential for NMS.
A congenital tricuspid-valve malformation classically linked to first-trimester lithium exposure; warrants fetal echocardiography.
Elevated prolactin from D2 blockade, causing menstrual changes, galactorrhea or sexual dysfunction; prominent with risperidone/paliperidone.
Serious birth defects of the brain or spine; a key teratogenic risk of valproate in pregnancy.
Damage to peripheral nerves causing numbness, tingling or weakness; phenelzine can cause it via vitamin B6 depletion.
Flushing, throbbing headache, nausea, vomiting and palpitations when alcohol is taken with disulfiram; deliberately aversive.
New compulsive urges (gambling, spending, binge-eating, hypersexuality) linked to dopamine partial agonists like aripiprazole.
A sudden rise in eye pressure damaging the optic nerve; topiramate can precipitate it with eye pain and blurred vision.
Abrupt, severe opioid withdrawal triggered when an antagonist or partial agonist (buprenorphine, naltrexone) displaces a full agonist.
Physiological adaptation to a drug so that stopping causes withdrawal; a benzodiazepine and opioid class risk.
A diminishing effect from the same dose over time, prompting escalation; seen with benzodiazepines and opioids.
Redirecting a prescribed controlled drug to non-medical use or sale; a risk with stimulants and some anticholinergics.
Low blood sodium; SSRIs and carbamazepine can cause it via SIADH, risking confusion and seizures.
Inappropriate ADH release causing water retention and dilutional hyponatremia; an SSRI and carbamazepine adverse effect.
Excessive dilute urination from ADH deficiency or resistance; lithium causes the nephrogenic (ADH-resistant) form.
A dangerous hyperglycemic emergency with ketosis and acidosis; olanzapine can precipitate new-onset diabetes and DKA.
A muscle enzyme that rises markedly in NMS from muscle breakdown; useful for diagnosis and monitoring.
Chronic, excessive, hard-to-control worry about everyday matters with restlessness, tension and poor sleep, most days for months.
Recurrent unexpected panic attacks (surges of fear with chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea) plus persistent worry about further attacks.
A marked, persistent fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social or performance situations, leading to dread or avoidance.
Intrusive distressing obsessions and repetitive compulsions done to reduce anxiety; needs higher SSRI doses and longer trials.
A trauma disorder with re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood and hyperarousal after a threatening event.
A depressive episode of at least 2 weeks with low mood or anhedonia plus sleep, appetite, energy and concentration changes.
A mood disorder with episodes of mania or hypomania (bipolar I/II) alternating with depression.
A period of abnormally elevated or irritable mood with increased energy lasting at least a week; defines bipolar I.
A milder, shorter form of mania without marked impairment or psychosis; a hallmark of bipolar II.
A chronic psychotic disorder with positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations), negative symptoms and cognitive impairment.
A loss of contact with reality featuring delusions, hallucinations or disorganized thinking.
Hallucinations or delusions in Parkinson disease; treat with agents that least worsen motor symptoms (quetiapine, clozapine, pimavanserin).
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and cognition from illness, drugs or withdrawal; find and correct the cause.
Progressive decline in memory and cognition impairing daily function; Alzheimer disease is the commonest cause.

aliases: behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia
def: Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia: agitation, aggression, wandering, delusions, hallucinations and disturbed sleep.
Heightened arousal with motor restlessness and rising hostility that can progress to aggression; de-escalate first, medicate for safety.
A problematic pattern of alcohol use with craving, loss of control, tolerance and withdrawal despite harm.
Compulsive opioid use with tolerance, withdrawal and continued use despite harm; treated with agonist therapy.
Nicotine dependence with compulsive tobacco use and withdrawal; pharmacotherapy plus counselling roughly triples quit rates.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity beginning in childhood; stimulants are the mainstay.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity, with daytime consequences; CBT-I is first-line.
A major depressive episode arising during pregnancy or after childbirth.
A psychiatric emergency of acute psychosis and mood disturbance soon after childbirth, requiring urgent admission.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness with sudden sleep attacks, sometimes with cataplexy.
A syndrome of marked motor and behavioural disturbance: immobility, mutism, posturing, or excited overactivity.
A disorder combining features of schizophrenia with prominent mood episodes; psychosis also occurs apart from the mood symptoms.
Depression that has not responded to adequate trials of two or more antidepressants.
Depression with mood reactivity plus increased sleep and appetite, leaden fatigue and rejection sensitivity; classically MAOI-responsive.
A prolonged or repeated seizure without recovery between; a neurologic emergency for which IV lorazepam is first-line.
Severe alcohol withdrawal with confusion, autonomic instability, tremor and hallucinations; potentially fatal.
An acute thiamine-deficiency triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia and ataxia; give thiamine before glucose.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; longer half-lives mean slower clearance and milder discontinuation.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance effect against tolerability.
An inactive drug the body converts into its active form, e.g., lisdexamfetamine or clorazepate.
A pharmacologically active compound the body makes from a drug, which can prolong or add to its effects.
Extensive breakdown of an oral drug by gut and liver before it reaches circulation; bypassed by patch or sublingual routes.
The point where drug intake and elimination balance, giving stable levels; usually reached after about five half-lives.
A larger initial dose to reach therapeutic levels quickly, e.g., valproate loading in acute mania.
A drug that binds a receptor but activates it only partly, stabilizing signalling; e.g., aripiprazole, buprenorphine.
A drug that fully activates a receptor with no ceiling, e.g., methadone at the opioid receptor.
When a drug induces the enzymes that clear it, so its own levels fall over weeks; classically carbamazepine.
A drug that speeds liver enzymes so co-medications clear faster and drop in level, e.g., carbamazepine or phenobarbital.
A drug that slows liver enzymes so co-medications accumulate to higher, potentially toxic levels, e.g., fluvoxamine, fluoxetine.
A liver enzyme that metabolizes many psychotropics; genetics and inhibitors like fluoxetine and paroxetine cause variable levels.
A major drug-metabolizing enzyme; inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, grapefruit) raise substrate levels, inducers lower them.
A liver enzyme induced by cigarette smoking and inhibited by fluvoxamine; key for clozapine and olanzapine levels.
A liver conjugation step without CYP oxidation; benzos cleared this way (lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam) are safer in liver disease.
A drug-free interval before starting an interacting agent, e.g., about 2 weeks (5 for fluoxetine) before an MAOI.
The blood-level range between effective and toxic; narrow-window drugs like lithium and TCAs need monitored levels.
A slow-release injected antipsychotic given every few weeks to a month to support adherence.
A restricted-distribution safety program with mandatory monitoring for high-risk drugs, e.g., esketamine.
Blocking the transporter that clears a neurotransmitter from the synapse, raising its levels; the basis of SSRIs and SNRIs.
Blockade of dopamine D2 receptors, the core antipsychotic mechanism and the source of EPS and prolactin elevation.
A drug blocking the NMDA glutamate receptor (ketamine/esketamine, memantine), triggering a glutamate surge and rapid effects.
A hypnotic that blocks wake-promoting orexin instead of enhancing GABA, e.g., lemborexant and daridorexant; non-dependence-forming.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine by blocking its vesicular transporter; used for tardive dyskinesia (e.g., tetrabenazine).
A monoamine oxidase inhibitor that raises serotonin, norepinephrine and dopamine; demands tyramine-diet and washout precautions.
A drug that raises acetylcholine by blocking its breakdown; used in Alzheimer dementia (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine).
A drug stimulating central alpha-2 receptors to reduce sympathetic drive (clonidine, guanfacine); abrupt stop causes rebound hypertension.
An alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist; prazosin uses it for PTSD nightmares, and it underlies orthostasis and priapism.
A beta-adrenergic antagonist; propranolol treats akathisia, tremor and performance anxiety but can trigger bronchospasm.
An H1-receptor blocker whose sedating effect underlies hypnotic and anxiolytic uses (hydroxyzine, doxepin, mirtazapine).
Blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors; treats acute dystonia but causes dry mouth, constipation, retention and confusion.
Blockade of carbonic anhydrase (topiramate) causing paresthesia, metabolic acidosis, kidney stones and glaucoma.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor: first-line antidepressants and anxiolytics that raise synaptic serotonin.
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (venlafaxine, duloxetine); dose-dependent noradrenergic effects and blood-pressure rise.
An older antidepressant class; effective but anticholinergic, sedating and cardiotoxic, causing wide QRS and arrhythmia in overdose.
A GABA-A-enhancing sedative-anxiolytic; effective but causes tolerance, dependence and respiratory depression with opioids or alcohol.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at the benzo site; for short-term insomnia, can cause complex sleep behaviours.
A 'typical' antipsychotic (haloperidol, chlorpromazine): a potent D2 blocker with high EPS risk.
An 'atypical' antipsychotic with combined serotonin-dopamine action: less EPS but often more metabolic side effects.
A steroid that modulates GABA-A receptors; the class of rapid postpartum-depression drugs brexanolone and zuranolone.
Treating opioid use disorder with a maintenance opioid (methadone, buprenorphine) to prevent withdrawal and craving.
A drug with legally restricted prescribing due to abuse or dependence potential, e.g., stimulants and benzodiazepines.
The strongest regulatory drug warning, highlighting a serious or life-threatening risk.
Adding a second agent (e.g., lithium or an atypical antipsychotic) to boost a partially effective antidepressant.
Gradually lowering one drug while raising its replacement to switch safely, e.g., alprazolam onto clonazepam.
Gradual dose reduction to avoid withdrawal, rebound or seizures when stopping a drug.
One mirror-image form of a molecule; purified single enantiomers include escitalopram, esketamine and armodafinil.
